1.texto_original: Now when Solomon had made an end of praying, the fire came down from heaven, and consumed the burnt-offering and the sacrifices; and the glory of Jehovah filled the house.
1.normal:
Devotional Study of 2 Chronicles 7:1

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"Now when Solomon had made an end of praying, the fire came down from heaven, and consumed the burnt-offering and the sacrifices; and the glory of Jehovah filled the house."
1.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of 2 Chronicles 7:1

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"Now when Solomon had made an end of praying, the fire came down from heaven, and consumed the burnt-offering and the sacrifices; and the glory of Jehovah filled the house."
1.ninos:
🌌 The Great Beginning! 🌍

Hello, universe explorers! 🚀 Imagine: before there was anything... not even time! ⏰... God decided to CREATE EVERYTHING! ✨

With a powerful "LET THERE BE!" 🤓, God made appear from absolute nothing ✨ the blue skies full of twinkling stars ⭐ and the Earth with mountains 🏔️, rivers 🌊 and animals 🐶. Like the greatest magic ever! 🪄

God is the most amazing artist 🎨 and everything is His masterpiece. Including YOU! 😊 When you see the clouds ☁️ or play in the park 🌳, remember: it all started with Gods creative love! ❤️

"Now when Solomon had made an end of praying, the fire came down from heaven, and consumed the burnt-offering and the sacrifices; and the glory of Jehovah filled the house."

3.texto_original: And all the children of Israel looked on, when the fire came down, and the glory of Jehovah was upon the house; and they bowed themselves with their faces to the ground upon the pavement, and worshipped, and gave thanks unto Jehovah, [saying], For he is good; for his lovingkindness [endureth] for ever.

7.texto_original: Moreover Solomon hallowed the middle of the court that was before the house of Jehovah; for there he offered the burnt-offerings, and the fat of the peace-offerings, because the brazen altar which Solomon had made was not able to receive the burnt-offering, and the meal-offering, and the fat.

"And on the three and twentieth day of the seventh month he sent the people away unto their tents, joyful and glad of heart for the goodness that Jehovah had showed unto David, and to Solomon, and to Israel his people."
10.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of 2 Chronicles 7:10

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"And on the three and twentieth day of the seventh month he sent the people away unto their tents, joyful and glad of heart for the goodness that Jehovah had showed unto David, and to Solomon, and to Israel his people."

🌌 The Great Beginning! 🌍

Hello, universe explorers! 🚀 Imagine: before there was anything... not even time! ⏰... God decided to CREATE EVERYTHING! ✨

With a powerful "LET THERE BE!" 🤓, God made appear from absolute nothing ✨ the blue skies full of twinkling stars ⭐ and the Earth with mountains 🏔️, rivers 🌊 and animals 🐶. Like the greatest magic ever! 🪄

God is the most amazing artist 🎨 and everything is His masterpiece. Including YOU! 😊 When you see the clouds ☁️ or play in the park 🌳, remember: it all started with Gods creative love! ❤️

"But if ye turn away, and forsake my statutes and my commandments which I have set before you, and shall go and serve other gods, and worship them;"

20.texto_original: then will I pluck them up by the roots out of my land which I have given them; and this house, which I have hallowed for my name, will I cast out of my sight, and I will make it a proverb and a byword among all peoples.
20.normal:
Devotional Study of 2 Chronicles 7:20

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"then will I pluck them up by the roots out of my land which I have given them; and this house, which I have hallowed for my name, will I cast out of my sight, and I will make it a proverb and a byword among all peoples."
20.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of 2 Chronicles 7:20

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"then will I pluck them up by the roots out of my land which I have given them; and this house, which I have hallowed for my name, will I cast out of my sight, and I will make it a proverb and a byword among all peoples."

22.texto_original: And they shall answer, Because they forsook Jehovah, the God of their fathers, who brought them forth out of the land of Egypt, and laid hold on other gods, and worshipped them, and served them: therefore hath he brought all this evil upon them.
